data.title: BNY Mellon Small Cap Value Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

“In our view, a cautiously optimistic outlook prevails, with US equities and selective international markets potentially offering opportunities amidst a volatile macro environment” Equity markets were generally higher across developed and emerging markets as the US retreated on its harshest tariff threats and signs the Federal Reserve (Fed) may begin cutting rates as inflation stabilized. Markets largely looked past geopolitical uncertainty in June as Israel launched surprise airstrikes on Iran’s nuclear sites, prompting retaliatory attacks and US strikes in support of Israel before a fragile ceasefire contained further escalation. The S&P 500® Index (SP500)(SPX) increased 10.94% for the quarter. Within the index, 8 of 11 Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) equity sectors finished in positive territory. The strongest performers were information technology and communication services.

Growth stocks outperformed value stocks, while large-cap outperformed small-cap. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the US and Canada, was higher by 11.78%. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index was higher by 11.99%.

In the US, equity market performance was higher in the second quarter of 2025 with the Dow Jones Industrial Average advancing by 5.46% and the Nasdaq 100 advancing by 17.86%. US fixed income markets were also higher for the quarter. First quarter GDP contracted by 0.3%, the first decline since 2022, due to a surge in pre- tariff imports. Despite this, the labor market remained resilient, with job growth moderating but consistently above expectations. Inflation appeared contained, with core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE), the Fed’s preferred measure, easing from 2.7% to 2.5% in May before rising slightly to 2.8% in June, still below forecasts. The Fed took no further rate actions in the quarter, although Vice Chair Michelle Bowman signaled openness to rate cuts as early as July amid softening job data and subdued inflationary impact from tariffs. Consumer sentiment rebounded from historic lows, while political developments—most notably President Trump’s evolving tariff strategy and the advancement of the “One Big Beautiful Bill”—added fiscal and policy uncertainty heading into the second half of the year.

In developed markets, equity markets were broadly higher. In the Eurozone, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut rates by 25 basis points (BPs) twice as inflation continued to moderate. The most recent inflation reading (May) came in at 1.9% relative to 2.2% in April and below the ECB’s long-term target of 2%. First quarter GDP grew at an annualized rate of 1.2%, effectively flat relative to the prior quarter. Despite growth resilience, the European Commission lowered its full-year forecast due to US-EU trade tensions. The UK had a first quarter GDP surge of 0.7%, the fastest in the Group of Seven (G7), which was partly driven by front-loaded activity ahead of new tariffs and tax changes. The Bank of England (BOE) cut rates by 25 bps in May before pausing in June amid lingering inflation concerns. In Japan, inflation spiked early in the quarter due to food and tariff-driven import costs but later moderated to 1.9%. The Bank of Japan (BOJ) left policy unchanged across both its meetings and announced a slower pace of bond purchase tapering beginning in 2026, aiming to stabilize bond market volatility.

QUARTERLY PERFORMANCE

BNY Mellon Small Cap Value Fund outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Value Index, during the second quarter of 2025 (based on gross-of-fees performance). From a sector perspective, industrials and energy contributed to relative results, while materials and communication services detracted.

SECTOR REVIEW

Positive Impacts

Industrials: Industrials was a lead contributing sector over the quarter. Out-of-index aerospace and defense company BWX Technologies, Inc. (BWXT) returned positively due to increasing momentum in the nuclear sector as a result of recent executive orders intended to revive the US nuclear industry, where BWX has high exposure to both government and commercial nuclear development.

Energy: Energy had overall mixed results, but a robust performance by an American nuclear fuel company kept the sector in positive territory. Centrus Energy Corp. (LEU) was a key contributor owing to first quarter results and continued robust demand for nuclear power across the US.

Negative Impacts

Materials: Despite positive absolute performance, an underweight kept the strategy in negative relative territory. Zero-weight in containers and packaging, which outperformed, and holdings in the out-of-index construction materials subsectors were main detracting factors.

Communication services: Communication services also detracted from relative returns as Starz Entertainment Corp was spun-out from Lionsgate Entertainment in early May 2025. Shares have performed well but may remain volatile given the newness of the company and impacts from transaction-related dynamics.

STOCK REVIEW1

Key Contributors

Centrus Energy Corp. Class A: Centrus Energy Corp. was a key contributor owing to first quarter results and continued robust demand for nuclear power across the US.

Magnite, Inc. (MGNI): Magnite announced robust first-quarter results with solid performance in its DV+ product and continued momentum in CTV, as well as a partnership announcement in late May with Amazon Publisher Services. We believe that Magnite, Inc. would see a benefit if the Department of Justice is successful in their antitrust litigation against Google (GOOG)(GOOGL). Additionally, Magnite’s partnership with Amazon allows it access to Amazon connected TV inventory.

nLIGHT, Inc. (LASR): Positive performance by nLight was driven by a first quarter earnings report that beat revenue expectations due to exceptional results in the aerospace and defense segment and management called out increased confidence in their second quarter outlook. The stock rose with defense suppliers as the outlook for defense spending for the US and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) allies improves.

Key Detractors

Starz Entertainment Corp (STRZ): Starz Entertainment Corp was spun-out from Lionsgate Entertainment in early May 2025. Shares have performed well but may remain volatile given the newness of the company and impacts from transaction-related dynamics.

Crescent Energy Company (CRGY): Crescent Energy experienced headwinds as oil prices fell sharply in April due to increased global supply and concerns about weakening demand.

Globus Medical Inc (GMED): Shares of Globus fell after the first quarter report missed consensus expectations driven by disappointing revenue and earnings-per-share (EPS) figures, lowered EPS guidance, and ongoing integration and supply-chain issues. Softer enabling technology deal closures were also a factor in underperformance.

1Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

MARKET OUTLOOK

In 2025, the US and global equity markets are navigating a complex landscape shaped by shifting monetary policies, trade uncertainties and technological disruption. Interest rates are expected to decline gradually, with the Federal Reserve projected to cut rates by 25 basis points per quarter starting in the third quarter of 2025, potentially lowering the federal funds rate to around 3% by year-end. This easing could help support equity valuations by reducing borrowing costs, particularly for small-cap stocks, which are more sensitive to interest rate changes. However, persistent trade policy volatility, driven by tariff negotiations, could elevate inflation risks, potentially tempering the pace of rate cuts. Despite these headwinds, global economic growth is expected to remain resilient, with the US economy growing at 0-1% and avoiding recession, while Europe and emerging markets like India benefit from accommodative policies and domestic growth drivers.

Corporate earnings were poised to be a key driver of equity performance, as US S&P 500 earnings growth had come in at 12.7% in the first quarter but are now projected to fall to approximately 5% for 2025 according to FactSet. Earnings revisions for US companies have recently trended downward, reflecting caution due to trade uncertainties, while non-US developed markets show more stable earnings outlooks. Despite the “Magnificent Seven” mega-cap tech stocks seeing their earnings growth decelerate, they are still outpacing the broader market of the “remaining” S&P 493 stocks currently. Internationally, Europe’s forward 12-month earnings growth of over 7% and EPS nearing new highs is encouraging, but it appears it may be peaking and turning lower.

Large-cap stocks, particularly in the US, remain favored due to their earnings resilience and exposure to AI- related growth, but we believe small-cap stocks present compelling opportunities as valuations trade at a sizable discount to fair value. Small caps are likely to benefit from lower interest rates and potential deregulation, which could catalyze a rebound, especially in cyclical sectors like industrials and financials.

However, their performance hinges on economic stability and tariff resolutions, as they are more exposed to domestic economic fluctuations. Conversely, large caps, especially tech-heavy names, may face risks from elevated valuations and potential AI sector corrections, as seen in early 2025 with the sell-off following DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) disruptive AI model announcement. A balanced approach favoring both growth and value stocks across regions may help mitigate risks while capturing upside.

Artificial intelligence continues to be a transformative force, driving productivity gains and reshaping equity market dynamics. The AI sector, while still concentrated in US mega-caps, is broadening as adoption spreads across industries like healthcare and industrials, with Asia (notably Taiwan and India) emerging as potential diversification opportunities. However, concerns about overinvestment in AI infrastructure, highlighted by the market’s reaction to DeepSeek’s cost-effective model, underscore risks of a potential bubble. For 2025, AI’s productivity benefits are expected to bolster corporate margins, but investors should remain selective, focusing on companies with strong fundamentals and reasonable valuations to navigate volatility. Overall, in our view, a cautiously optimistic outlook prevails, with US equities and selective international markets potentially offering opportunities amidst a volatile macro environment, provided actively-managed risks are tied to policy uncertainty and AI-driven disruptions.

Risks

Equities are subject to market, market sector, market liquidity, issuer, and investment style risks, to varying degrees. Small and midsized company stocks tend to be more volatile and less liquid than larger company stocks as these companies are less established and have more volatile earnings histories. Investing in foreign denominated and/or domiciled securities involves special risks, including changes in currency exchange rates, political, economic, and social instability, limited company information, differing auditing and legal standards, and less market liquidity. These risks generally are greater with emerging market countries.

Artificial intelligence refers to computer systems that can perform tasks typically requiring human intelligence, such as visual perception, speech recognition, decision making and language translation.

As of 6/30/25, the fund’s top ten holdings were: First Horizon (FHN) 2.39%, Genius Sports (GENI) 1.97%, Seacoast Banking Of Florida (SBCF) 1.72%, Magnite (MGNI) 1.65%, BWX Technologies 1.62%, SLM (SLM) 1.55%, Columbia Banking System (COLB) 1.55%, Texas Capital Bancshares (TCBI) 1.51%, TXNM Energy (TXNM) 1.42%, Gates Industrial (GTES) 1.40%,

As of 6/30/25, the companies mentioned represented 5.49% of the fund’s portfolio in the aggregate. The holdings listed should not be considered recommendations to buy or sell a particular security. Other holdings may not have performed as well as some of those listed herein. Portfolio composition is subject to change at any time.

It includes those Russell 2000 companies with lower growth earning potential as defined by Russell's leading style methodology. data.title: Viking Holdings: Valuation Is Rich
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Viking Holdings (NYSE:VIK) previously with a hold rating, as I was still worried about the FY26 outlook, in particular the pricing trends and net yield growth. While the latest 2Q25 results were strong, the core question remains around FY26 pricing momentum. Bookings are clearly healthy, but with net yield growth still trailing historical levels, I see limited scope for further multiple expansion until there is clearer evidence of re-acceleration.

2Q25 earnings

VIK’s 2Q25 results showed the strength. Total revenue grew a staggering 18.5% y/y to $1.88 billion, driven by both higher capacity and pricing power. Capacity increased 8.8% y/y on fleet growth, while net yield increased 8% y/y. The Ocean segment was noteworthy, delivering a 12% y/y net yield gain in 1H25. Meanwhile, the River segment, which had shown weakness in 1Q25, rebounded meaningfully as sailing season ramped up, with 1H25 net yield up 6.9% y/y. Strong revenue momentum translated into earnings growth, with adj. EBITDA up 28.5% y/y to $633 million and adj. EPS up to $0.99.

Demand Outlook Strengthens, Easing Prior Concerns

The outlook for the current year has improved considerably since my last update. Back then (in May), VIK had already de-risked most of FY25 with 92% of capacity sold, but it surely doesn’t hurt to know that management has managed to push that figure to 96%, which basically means VIK has effectively sold out for the year. When combined with the pricing (net yield) that continues to run ~7% y/y higher, this level of pre-booking has essentially removed virtually all uncertainty I had for FY25, and in fact, demonstrated that consumer demand remains strong.

More importantly, the 2Q25 results also address the “lack of visibility” that I was worried about in May. To be specific, VIK's booked position (percentage of total available cruise capacity that has already been sold for a future season at a specific point in time) for the 2026 season has surged to 55% of capacity, which is a very big improvement from the 37% figure seen in 1Q25. I remind readers that this increase is based on an even larger capacity (9% y/y capacity growth), which means VIK is not only selling further ahead but also successfully filling an even larger fleet well in advance. The strength becomes even clearer when viewed in dollar terms. Advanced bookings for 2026 now stand at $3.9 billion, representing a 13% increase vs. the same point last year for the 2025 season. This shows that top-line demand is growing at a double-digit clip and is easily absorbing new supply. The latter is important because it justifies VIK’s expansion strategy. Management commentary was also very encouraging. They mentioned that June and July saw outstanding levels of bookings, with the momentum continuing into August. In my view, this does suggest that the demand surge is not a blip since it has been sustained through the peak selling window.

Shifting focus to the River segment, there has also been an obvious turnaround success. After a soft start earlier in the year, advanced bookings per passenger cruise days [PCD] for FY26 are now pacing at ~6% y/y. This is a sharp turnaround and points to pricing power returning as the booking curve for the main European sailing season matures (i.e., as consumers finalize plans and book their trips). Note: The European sailing season is between April and October.

FY26 Pricing Remains the Key Question Mark

Despite the encouraging booking volumes, the key issue I highlighted previously (the softer pricing trend for FY26) is still bugging me, and I think that puts a limit to the upside. For perspective, overall advanced bookings per PCD for 2026 are tracking at +4% y/y, essentially unchanged from May. This is well below the ~7% pricing growth VIK had achieved for FY25 at the same stage. This is also below VIK’s historical net yield trajectory. While management reiterated confidence in ultimately reaching mid-single-digit price increases for the year, the lack of visible momentum (pricing still at ~4% y/y) leaves me concerned that strong volumes are coming at the expense of softer pricing.

In fact, if you look deeper, the flat 4% overall pricing figure masks two contrasting trends. Firstly, river pricing has actually accelerated, but this was offset by a slowdown in the ocean segment. For ocean cruises, advanced bookings per PCD eased to 3.7% y/y, down from 5.0% in my previous update. Relative to River, Ocean is a much bigger segment (VIK main growth driver), and hence, this makes the entire pricing concern of mine even more important.

My belief is that this creates a new layer of complexity/uncertainty. Even as management suggested they could have been more aggressive on pricing, the moderation signals otherwise. This comes at a time when VIK is showing good booking strength. If VIK tries to raise prices and booking momentum stalls, it will very likely strike fear in investors that volume is indeed growing at the expense of pricing.

As such, until there is clearer evidence that the FY26 pricing curve is re-accelerating back toward the historical algorithm, I remain cautious.

Valuation

Anyway, even if I thought the fundamentals were great and wanted to upgrade to buy, VIK’s valuation has soared to levels beyond my imagination. Since May, the forward EBITDA multiple has surged to 15x (from low teens). Inserting that valuation into my previous model (anchored against FY26 consensus EBITDA estimates), this implies a share price target of $58, which is near where VIK is trading today. I think it's hard to imagine valuation going any higher from here since this is near the all-time high and is at near the widest margin vs. peers’ valuation (peers include Royal Caribbean Cruises, Carnival Corp, and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings). Hence, EBITDA needs to go up to justify a higher share price, but that is where I am uncertain. If pricing continues to decelerate with bookings growth, it limits VIK’s ability to accelerate growth. Therefore, I am still not willing to upgrade to a buy.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating for VIK. The near-term setup is strong, with FY25 essentially sold out, FY26 bookings ahead of pace, and River segment pricing improving. However, the lack of acceleration in consolidated FY26 pricing, particularly in the Ocean segment, still makes me cautious. Coupled with a valuation now at ~15x forward EBITDA—well above peers and near historical peaks—I believe risk/reward remains balanced.

data.title: Light At The End Of The AI Bottleneck: Pre-Commercial Poet Technologies Stock Is Buyable
data.description:
Numbers, usually an investor’s best friend, can also become an obstacle.

To a Seeking Alpha investor audience, I’d be preaching to the choir if I explained the importance of numbers (relating to earnings, financial condition, etc.) such as those reported regularly by publicly traded companies.

I’ve been screening for stocks since the 1980s, when the program was on a floppy disk in the pc’s A drive. Data, stored on a second floppy, went into the B drive. It was cool to watch the A- and B-drive lights flash back and forth as the computer read alternately from the program and the data.

(Footnote for history buffs or trivia fans: When hard drives were added, they were labeled C. Floppy disks are now long gone. This is why computers today have a C drive (and D, etc. if one programmatically partitions them), but nothing for A or B.)

I even published two books, one on fundamentals-based screening and the other on valuation. (I’m not telling you to buy them – they might be a bit dated by now.)

I say this so you understand who is speaking here as I, a long-time fundamental value guy and quant, tell you, as I have for other early stage companies, that I’m going to rate shares of POET Technologies (NASDAQ: POET), a pre-commercial photonics company that adds light (photonics) to conventional semiconductor-based electronic transmission, a “Buy.”

I’m not going to address Graham and Dodd, Warren Buffett, or anybody like that (although I’m well-studied in all of them).

Instead, I’ll cite an important guru you may not know of. His name is Craveth Read. He was an 18th century British logician. His famous adage (often erroneously attributed to John Maynard Keynes) is as follows:

“[I]t is a mistake to aim at an unattainable precision. It is better to be vaguely right than exactly wrong.”

Investment analysis of POET based on any discussion of current or historical numbers for is “exactly wrong.” Such considerations aim at “unattainable precision.”

That’s so for pre-commercial POET. It’s also so for minimal startup-level revenue companies. We see these in quantum computing companies, and many in the biotech field.

This does not mean we should plunge wily nilly into any such company. Analysis is still critical. But we need a different approach, a qualitative analysis.

It’s how value-guru William Miller chose to invest big in Amazon (AMZN) stock almost from day one.

That said…

For Those Who Crave Ratios, I’ll Invent One Right Now: S/R

S stands for Story.

This can be about technology. It can be about a new business model. It can be anything like this. The key is that it be big and credible.

R stands for Risk.

This isn’t about risk in the abstract. All such companies, by their incipient nature, are at the highest level of the risk scale. It’s really about your personal risk tolerance.

For you, R is a two-part question.

  1. Do you want to assume this kind of risk at all?
  2. If you are willing to assume such risk, think about position sizing. It would likely be very bad to put 25% of your portfolio in a stock like POET. A stake below 5% (perhaps closer to 1%-2%) would seem more appropriate for many investors.

Given that risk-tolerance is personal to you, I’ll focus here on Story.

Introducing POET – What It’s About and What It Does

“Let there be light,” pertained (biblically speaking) to the universe eons ago.

POET is now bringing it to semiconductors — chips, those electronic gizmos developed in the mid-20th century and with which we now can’t seem to live without.

Semiconductors make much of today’s technology possible. Lots of data and/or logic are crammed onto these tiny things. They involve etching complex pathways onto thin wafers. Electrical-conducting substances go into those pathways. So, too, do itty bitty logic processors, and gizmos to prevent them from overheating.

Absent semiconductors, who knows how much voluminous wiring etc. we’d need to in order to bulk up even the simplest among todays’ devices.

If we could do everything imaginable with electrical current, we’d be golden — and POET would not exist or it would be doing something very different from what it actually does. But…

Light (Photonics) Has Properties Electronics Can’t Match

The semiconductor folks are quite candid about things photonics can do that traditional semiconductors can’t. Semiengineering.com tells us what’s great about photonics:

* Speed: Nothing travels faster than photons, so using light pulses to transmit information is ideal for speed-centric applications.

* Bandwidth: The characteristics of light (wide frequency range, multiple wavelengths, minimal interference, low signal loss) enable immense amounts of information to be transmitted at once.

* Energy: Photonics-based systems are typically very energy efficient due to the minimal loss of signal over long distances.

* Size: Photonic components like sensors, microLEDs, waveguides, and integrated circuits can be made highly compact to minimize the physical footprint of various technologies.

Interesting list. But it’s not qualitatively surprising. These all sound like areas in which modern chip-based electronics are superior to pre-semiconductor electrical transmission.

So, you may rightfully ask, why do we need photonics. Why can’t we just make electronic chips even more powerful?

I think it boils down to the reality that electricity (based on movement of electrons) and light (based on movement of protons) are two different things each with its own set of properties.

For more, we’d have to ask the creator of the universe why it was set up that way. Since I am not the creator, nor in direct communication with said being, I’ll have to stop here and leave it at that. Put another way…

It’s the creator’s universe, and we just live in it — and cope as best we can.

And if we are gong to do our best, we’ll need to incorporate photonics to enhance modern electronics. We need to call on light to take us where electronics alone can’t go.

That’s hard, because…

From a Manufacturing Standpoint, Photonics is Much More Demanding Than Electronics

As challenging as it can be to put metallic circuits onto etched semiconductor wafers, etc., photonics is harder.

You see, electronic signals can move just as easily through all kinds of pathways. But light wants to travel in straight lines. That means waveguides (analogous to electronic circuits) have to work harder to direct photonic “currents” to go where they need to go.

Photonic circuits must be aligned with extreme precision, more so than already precise (by normal people standards) electronic chips.

Electronic signals can be amplified. Light signals cannot. So, with photonics you get one chance to get the right intensity.

Photonics is far more temperature sensitive than electronics (the latter’s sensitivity already seems quite high to us mortals).

There are also differences between electrical and optical communication methods, production costs, and commercial scalability.

This sounds frustrating. It seems as if we can’t do our best to work with both electronics and light unless we take on a lot of extra cost and hassle.

How good would it be if we could work with light as we do with electronics, or if we could “semiconductorize” photonics, to produce light-handling gizmos at the wafer-scale we’ve come to know and love with semiconductors.

Good news!

It looks like we’re getting there…

POET’s Optical Interposer Aims at Heretofore Unprecedented Electronics-Photonics Integration

I’ve written much here extolling the virtues and challenges of combining electronics with photonics. Given all that, you’d have to expect that many companies – some of them quite large – would be working hard at this.

You’d be right. This is a busy area.

Companies in one aspect of this or another include Coherent (COHR), Cisco (CSCO), Intel (INTC), Broadcom (AVGO), and, Innolight (a Chinese company).

Wow. Would we want a pre-commercial company that has to compete with outfits like those? I wouldn’t. And I’m not suggesting you buy into any. POET isn’t actually a competitor to any of them.

POET is an enabler. Its optical interposer is actually a platform. Optical engines and transceivers, the things those companies make, are built from a platform such as the one POET is developing.

Phew. We can breathe now – or can we?

POET is not the only company working on such platforms. But here, at least, the rivals aren’t gigantic household names. But pros in the field know of them. Some names (none publicly traded) include Celestial AI, Ayar Labs, and Ranovus.

Ultimately, though, it’s not necessarily about big-name companies. As technology progresses, new names enter the field. And right now, POET has entered in a strong way.

There is one thing unique about POET. But it’s a big one…

It boils down to a label POET rightfully adopted for itself – “semiconductorization” of photonics. It’s about the way POET builds its platform.

The company…

integrates lasers, detectors, and electronic circuits all on a single chip-level module. This design could slash the cost and power consumption of optical transceivers used in data centers and telecom. POET’s technology aims to eliminate many of the traditional assembly steps. Rather than individually wiring components, Optical Interposer has them all “printed” together. The company has started sampling 100G/200G/400G fiber links and has a joint venture in Asia for manufacturing. If POET secures design wins with big network gear makers or cloud providers, it could scale up revenue quickly from a small base.

POET puts it this way:

The POET Optical Interposer™ utilizes a novel waveguide technology that allows the integration of electronic and photonic devices into a single multi-chip module. By applying advanced wafer-level semiconductor manufacturing techniques and novel packaging methods, POET’s Optical Interposer eliminates costly components, assembly, alignment and testing methods employed in conventional photonics solutions. In addition to lowering costs compared to conventional devices….

Interposers have been used for several years to allow electronic devices (microprocessors, memory devices, etc.) to communicate with each other via high-speed metal traces embedded in a semiconductor wafer. POET invented methods for embedding or placing photonic devices or features (lasers, detectors, waveguides, filters, etc.) within the interposer, allowing high-speed communication between electronic and photonic devices.

The POET Optical Interposer consists of layers of materials built on a standard semiconductor wafer of any size, using standard semiconductor (CMOS) processes. The bottom layers include the high-speed metal traces found in electronic interposers, while the top-most layers incorporate waveguides that guide and focus light, other passive devices (e.g., filters and gratings) and features which enable the accurate placement and integration of active devices.

Don’t sweat the details if you aren’t a hard-core tech person.

Here’s another, graphic, view.

Ultimately, you should focus on the overall themes of integration and easier manufacturing.

That’s POET’s unique calling card!

And as calling cards go, that’s a great one to have…

Why the World Needs What POET is Working to Supply

This is easy.

How often have you heard thoughtful commentators taking, and worrying, about the enormous power needs of AI? (And let’s add in quantum computing, crypto, factory automation, etc.)

We want things done quickly, incredibly quickly, so quickly we can’t even count the nanoseconds.

Occasional latency as you try to start a Netflix (NFLX) program can make it feel like steam is coming out of your ears.

Now imagine even tinier latency as a robo-taxi is trying to decide whether it can make safely make a left turn (across oncoming traffic) on a busy New York City two-way avenue. No, no, no to latency.

If you’re among those who think we’re over-investing in AI, data centers, etc. and that we’re already at or near where we need to be…

Seriously, try to find a time machine and hitch a ride back to, say, 1985.

Check out the supercomputers and super-networks of the era. I still remember a back-in-the-day analyst meeting with Cablevision, the cable TV firm that was acquired by Altice USA (ATUS) in 2016. I’m not sure who chaired the meeting, possibly the firm’s late founder Charles Dolan.

My most vivid memory was of Dolan (or whoever) suggesting it may not ever be possible to deliver movies to viewers to view when they wish because nobody would want to wait two hours or so for it to download. Maybe while you’re visiting the 1980s, you might try to sneak into that meeting!

Then, come home and do some AI stuff. (Try asking Perplexity AI to help you find good web authorities on complicated topics, as I often do.) Suddenly, you’ll notice it’s really slow, even the basic stuff. The more complex the task (for Perplexity, it could be Deep Research or Labs), the longer it takes. I rarely have the patience to use Labs.

Do you use AI for imaging or coding. Is it as fast as you want it to be? Reallly?

And in my 8/8/25 PLTR report, I described a simple inference experiment I did that shows how primitive today’s AI really is.

We’ve got bottlenecks that need clearing!

Folks, between continuing to develop AI, along with crypto etc., we need a scary amount of additional power. We need it at reasonable cost. And we need things to not overheat.

We need electronics and photonics to do more than just work together. We need them to work together well. And POET’s technology – its optical interposer – figures to play a big role in bursting through the bottlenecks that threaten to obstruct the progress we want and need.

Commercialization – Not Yet, But Prospects Coming into Focus

POET’s revenues aren’t quite at zero. But they’re darned close.

Sometimes the company will book modest revenues from non-recurring engineering services. Commercial revenue — from selling products — is actually zero.

But commercialization is getting closer. It’s not a snap-one’s-fingers now-we’re-doing-it thing. It’s a process.

POET is now focused on working with potential customers showing and qualifying its offerings, and establishing manufacturing arrangements.

Here’s how POET will fit into the overall marketplace:

This is a hard pill for many investors to swallow. We still use the future tense when discussing commerce.

But we know demand is strong.

So once commerce gets going, it’s likely to ramp up very quickly.

The Only Current Numbers Worth Even Barely Worth Looking At

At this stage in POET’s history, numbers are relevant for just one thing, to show whether or not POET is solvent.

The balance sheet shows the answer is “yes.”

A harder question is whether it can remain solvent going forward. It’ll take time for commercial revenues to catch up to and eventually surpass current and future expenses.

Every early stage company faces this same issue. And I give the same answer each time…

Capital markets will continue to supply as much as the company needs for as long as it needs it — so long as the markets believe in the viability and credibility of the business plans. I discuss this further in my 7/14/25 report on gene-editor CRSP.

The capital markets are showing themselves quite willing to support POET’s strategy. (See, for example, here, here, here, here, and here.)

Risk

As discussed above, POET is all the way at the top of the risk scale.

Being pre-commercial, the company stays alive thanks to its ability to have continually attracted outside capital. Such companies do so, and keep going like this, for as long as the capital markets believe in the credibility of the story.

POET is not alone in this. Much of biotech lives this way. A lot of AI firms still depend on outside capital. Quantum computing depends entirely on it.

Once upon a time, such companies wouldn’t even have had publicly traded stock. They’d have depended entirely on angel and/or venture capital financing.

That’s not so today. Now, many early stage investors are eager to cash out via IPO. And the equity markets are very accommodating.

We can like this. We can hate this.

No matter – This is how the world now works. All we can do is live in it, and decide for ourselves whether or not we wish to bear the risks of participating in this aspect of the equity market.

If POET’s technology doesn’t work, I can’t say what, if anything, would be left of the company.

What to do About POET Stock

This section is addressed ONLY to those who have made personal decisions to participate in POET’s portion of the risk scale. (I’m now talking to readers, who, if they reject the POET story, might wind up choosing a different comparably risky opportunity.)

Here’s the price chart. Treat this as a record showing how other risk-tolerant investors have been reacting to the POET story.

I’ve seen better. I’ve seen worse.

The 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) has been all over the place relative to the 50-day EMA. And the 10 just crossed below the 50. Not good. But it looks to be turning upward. So it may soon go back above the 50. For how long? Who knows.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) have often given mixed signals.

Both indicators measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

At this moment, CO is bullish. CMF is not. Both indicators bounced around over the past year. But CMF has spent more time in conspicuously bearish territory. CO, when it’s been bearish, has been only modestly so. More often, CO has ranged between neutral and positive.

My takeaway from CMF and CO: POET, for now, seems more an individual investor stock than something the big institutions love. But that’s understandable. The latter likely struggle more with pre-commercial situations.

If I were an institutional investor, POET would likely appeal to me more between 5pm and 9am, when I’m on my own time and investing for myself.

Between 9am and 5pm (or whatever hours one works), I’d be in a professional fiduciary capacity investing other people’s money. That would likely give me pause, unless I’m working on a very specialized high-risk-tolerant account.

Lets face it. Sometimes, individual investors enjoy advantages over institutions!

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see POET as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market, and maybe even a year-ahead market if its starts commercializing as quickly as it now expects. (That could really wake up the institutions and do wonders for CMF.)

Also, be aware that Seeking Alpha Analyst Ahmed Abdelazim suggested on 8/21/25 that POET might become a takeover target. I don’t know. But I certainly won’t argue against that possibility.

As, essentially, a technology shop, I could easily see POET appealing to a larger company looking to buy rather than build (as many nowadays prefer). That’s especially so for POET considering its bottleneck-busting position within AI, etc.

I translate all this to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating POET as a ‘Buy’ for readers whose personal risk tolerances allow them to consider pre-commercial emerging tech.

data.title: FTHY: Worth Monitoring For Term Opportunities
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

First Trust High Yield Opportunities 2027 Term (NYSE:FTHY) is a fixed-income focused fund, mixing in senior loans and high yield bonds. In our prior update, we noted that the discount had narrowed, and that started to make it less appealing. A year later, the discount has narrowed even a touch further. That would mean that I'd continue to see it as a 'Hold' here, though with its liquidation set in around two years, there could still be an opportunity to hold or monitor this fund more closely.

FTHY Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.74
  • Discount/Premium: -3.05%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.26%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.80%
  • Leverage: 22.61%
  • Managed Assets: $716.481 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation around August 1st, 2027)

FTHY's investment objective is "to provide current income." They intend to do this by:

investing at least 80% of its Managed Assets in high yield debt securities of any maturity that are rated below investment grade at the time of purchase or unrated securities determined by the Advisor to be of comparable quality. High-yield debt securities include the U.S. and non-U.S. corporate debt obligations and senior, secured floating-rate loans ("Senior Loans").

This fund is leveraged, and that increases the fund's total expense ratio as the borrowings come with interest that needs to be paid. The fairly high operating expense gets pushed even higher to 3.26%. These borrowings are based on SOFR plus 1.05%, so they will see fluctuations as the Fed moves its benchmark rate.

On the other hand, given the fund's senior loan exposure, most of the fluctuations in the costs are absorbed. In other words, as rates rose, the underlying yield generated from the portfolio also increased, and vice versa.

Interestingly, they have taken their borrowings up since our prior update. They've even taken up the borrowings a bit since their latest annual report, which reflects the fiscal year ended May 31, 2025.

Discount Continues To Narrow

With FTHY being a fixed-income-oriented portfolio, it can make it more appropriate to compare its performance against fixed-income-focused ETFs as a barometer for how it has performed since our prior update. In this case, with its hybrid approach, the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) and the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). These are non-leveraged ETFs that can help provide some overall context to how FTHY is performing. In this case, the fund's total returns have outpaced both of those ETFs.

However, the fund's total NAV returns were a bit lower. The NAV per share declined a hair from $15.49 to $15.01, but the fund also paid $1.64 in distributions during this time. That would have brought its returns to positive territory.

However, in the last year, the fund's discount narrowed, so that's how we know the fund's total NAV returns would have been slightly less. We were sitting at a discount of -4.52%, and it has narrowed to -3.05%. In fact, the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially from where it was only a couple of years ago. In the last three years, the average discount comes in at -8.13%.

This could be partially because we have the anticipated liquidation date coming up in just under two years now. That is, the date the fund is set to liquidate because it is a term structured fund. First Trust has been exiting the CEF business, converting funds into ETFs, or divesting some funds to Aberdeen.

With that, I suspect they'll let this fund liquidate as expected or potentially try to get a vote to convert to an ETF. Something that other fund sponsors tend to do with their term structured funds, particularly Nuveen and Western Asset, is to convert them into perpetual funds. It makes sense. Why would they want to give up fee-generating AUM?

However, they generally do so with a tender offer at 100% of NAV for 100% of shares outstanding, which allows investors to get out of the funds if they choose. Some, those that have traded at premiums for regular periods of time, have put votes up to shareholders to switch to perpetual. That can also make sense, because when trading at a premium, one wouldn't want to liquidate, as it would mean an immediate loss.

Either way, investors should be able to realize the actual discount in this fund. That's why this fund would be worth holding or monitoring more closely for outsized opportunities such as the April discount that gapped wider before narrowing sharply.

The liquidation and realization of the fund's discount can provide guaranteed alpha relative to a non-term structured fund that invested similarly. Of course, that does not mean guaranteed returns; this is because the fund's underlying portfolio could perform weakly with NAV erosion that the distributions may not be able to offset.

Double-Digit Distribution Appealing But Regular Adjustments

FTHY helps to deliver a relatively high distribution yield, coming in at 10.26%, pushing it just into the double-digit rate level. On a NAV basis, the fund's distribution rate comes to 9.99%, so quite similar given the fairly negligible discount these days. That said, since the fund's existence, we've seen quite a few adjustments in the payout.

That isn't necessarily uncommon for a fund exposed to floating rate exposure in its underlying portfolio, but FTHY doesn't have that much in terms of senior loans—mostly enough, it would seem, to offset the borrowing cost fluctuations.

In looking at the fund's financial highlights, we can see that net investment income for the last few years has been fairly stable. In 2021 and 2022, it was quite a bit higher, and that could explain some of the increased payout they probably felt comfortable doing. Still, the fund has never really covered its distribution entirely from NII. In fact, it has always been rather low for a fixed-income fund.

The latest shows around 57% distribution coverage, similar to what we saw in the last two years. Even in the fiscal 2022 year, where NII was highest, relative to what they paid out during that period would have put NII coverage at ~69%.

This is where the NAV erosion can come in, as they overdistribute. It has also come in with a higher rate environment, pushing their underlying portfolio lower.

Given the lack of coverage in the distribution, it isn't too surprising to see return of capital make up a rather hefty portion of the distribution tax classification for the last couple of years.

FTHY's Portfolio

One place the fund could earn the distribution would be from its underlying portfolio, potentially experiencing appreciation. While the junk-rated securities that FTHY invests in are relatively less interest rate sensitive, they aren't entirely immune. The weighted average effective duration comes to 3.23 years. When rates change 1% up or down, the fund would be expected to move by 3.23% up or down.

Only 2.22% of the portfolio is rated as investment-grade, and at BBB- it is just barely.

In looking at average YTM, it also suggests that even with the expected upside of receiving par back at maturity, the 'yield' here wouldn't be enough. That's assuming they held every single position to maturity and there were no defaults or bankruptcies from the underlying holdings.

This means that while the fund delivers a strong distribution yield that might be appealing, we would expect to continue to see the NAV erode a bit going forward. That is another reason why we would like to see a wider discount before we'd consider this a 'Buy' again.

That said, if rates decline meaningfully in the next two years, that could help to limit some of the NAV erosion or even eliminate it, as the underlying portfolio could see some appreciation.

At the same time, if we are getting meaningful rate cuts, it could suggest that it is because we are in an economic slowdown. That would likely cause its lower credit quality-rated portfolio to see more defaults and bankruptcies. That, in turn, likely means that less of the portfolio would experience that face value payoff at maturity.

High yield spreads also remain quite low historically. We experienced a spike in April, but that has come right back down.

This is important because what it means is that investors are rather optimistic going forward. They are willing to accept lower and lower yields, despite the risks. It is a bit like a coiled spring, but certainly in a negative way.

With that, there isn't much margin of safety here, as a spike in spreads would see the underlying portfolio value drop meaningfully. The timing of the event might not be exactly predictable, but as we can see historically, these spikes do occur regularly. The spike in April was relatively mild and rather quick, but it was a bit of a taste. It becomes more important for FTHY the closer we get to the term date.

One of the ways to combat overall risk here for the fund is to invest in a diversified portfolio. It might be virtually all junk-rated, but it is highly diversified throughout many different industry exposures. The fund also lists nearly 300 different holdings, according to CEFConnect.

The top ten holdings don't necessarily make up overly concentrated positions. The largest being 2.39%, which is a bit on the higher end for a high-yield portfolio. With that said, those percentage allocations quickly decline from there for FTHY.

Some other funds that we cover, such as the BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund (DHF), could be a good example of being a bit more diversified. They have listed ~340 holdings on CEFConnect, but none of its holdings have over a 2% allocation, with the sixth largest position already sub-1%.

Conclusion

FTHY provides exposure to a hybrid fixed-income portfolio primarily in high-yield bonds but also carries a sleeve of senior loan exposure. That smaller sleeve can be helpful to balance the fund's borrowing costs for its leverage versus what the underlying portfolio can generate in income. The fund has an attractive distribution yield, though the payout is adjusted fairly regularly and the coverage in terms of income is low—and has been that way since the fund's inception.

With the termination expected in a couple of years, the fund is worth continuing to hold here or monitoring more closely for an entry point. That said, with the current discount, it does not provide an attractive level that one would want to initiate a position.

Despite the lackluster performance in July, 3 more Dividend Kings have pushed ahead of SPY year-to-date, bringing the total to 17.

Earnings Projections

Promising Kings in 2025

My article was published on July 22nd, and from July 23rd to August 21st, these 17 Kings had an average total return of -0.07%, compared to +0.21% for all 55 Dividend Kings and +0.73% for SPY. Not the best outcome, but these Kings were selected as long-term opportunities.

Dividend Yield Theory & Expected Rate of Return

I utilize Dividend Yield Theory as my primary valuation model for this analysis. Here is a brief explanation of how it works as well as my expected rate of return.

Dividend yield theory compares the forward dividend yield to the trailing 5-year average dividend yield to measure undervaluation. The expected long-term annualized rate of return is measured as follows:

Expected ROR = 3-5 year analyst earnings growth forecast * margin of safety + forward dividend yield + 5-year annualized return for fair value based on dividend yield theory

data.title: John Hancock Fundamental Large Cap Core Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a rocky start, U.S. stocks rose to a new high in the second quarter, buoyed by a 90-day pause on new import tariffs, progress on trade talks, resilient corporate earnings, continued economic growth, and stable inflation readings.
  • For the three months, the fund returned 8.35%, trailing the 10.94% result of the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500), (SPX) Index.
  • Security selection in the information technology sector notably detracted from the fund's results versus the index, with added pressure from stock picks in the consumer discretionary and healthcare sectors; conversely, investment choices in the financials sector gave a sizable boost to relative performance.

Market review and outlook

The U.S. stock market closed the second quarter at a record high. While proposed new import tariffs initially depressed returns, a subsequent 90-day pause on their implementation and progress on trade negotiations spurred a rebound in May and June. Despite uncertainty around tariff and tax policies, a late-period trade agreement between the United States and China, a ceasefire between Iran and Israel, and the decision by Canada to drop its digital services tax helped push the benchmark to a strong finish. Within the index, the information technology and communication services sectors led the way. Industrials and consumer discretionary stocks also were strong performers, while healthcare and energy stocks lagged.

We're cautiously optimistic about prospects for U.S. stocks, given progress in trade talks and potential changes in tax policy. Moreover, the U.S. job market remains robust, inflation seems subdued, and fears of an economic slowdown appear to be easing. However, many uncertainties remain, with the potential for more headline-driven market volatility.

Portfolio review

Security selection in the information technology sector notably detracted from the fund's performance versus the benchmark this period; stock picks and an underweight in the healthcare sector further hindered relative performance. Not owning semiconductor companies and index components NVIDIA Corp (NVDA). and Broadcom Inc (AVGO). hurt most, as robust demand for artificial intelligence chips fueled steep gains; however, these stocks didn't fit our valuation discipline. Shares of health insurer UnitedHealth Group (UNH) Inc. plunged, following disappointing financial results, driven by higher-than-expected costs in its Medicare Advantage business.

Conversely, stock picks in the financials sector boosted the fund's relative result. Avoiding conglomerate and index component Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B) helped most, as the stock fell following news that Warren Buffett would retire as CEO in 2025 and a report of a year-over-year decline in first-quarter profits. Elsewhere, an overweight in KLA Corp (KLAC)., which makes equipment and software to monitor the production of semiconductors, and a non-index position in semiconductor manufacturer Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) Company Ltd. each rallied sharply, buoyed by positive momentum for information technology stocks, easing trade tensions with China, and continued strong demand for their products.

Portfolio changes

During the quarter, we significantly boosted the fund's exposure to the healthcare sector, taking advantage of opportunities to buy competitively advantaged businesses with compelling long-term earnings growth prospects at attractive prices. We trimmed allocations to the consumer discretionary and industrials sectors.

This commentary reflects the views of the portfolio managers named and is subject to change as market and other conditions warrant. No forecasts are guaranteed. This commentary is provided for informational purposes only and is not an endorsement of any security, mutual fund, sector, or index. The subadvisor, the advisor (John Hancock Investment Management LLC), and their affiliates, employees, and clients, may hold or trade the securities mentioned in this commentary. Diversification does not guarantee a profit or eliminate the risk of a loss.

data.title: John Hancock Emerging Markets Equity Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund's benchmark—the MSCI (MSCI) Emerging Markets Index—recovered from an early downturn to finish the second quarter with a gain.
  • The fund produced a positive total return, but it trailed the benchmark.
  • Stock selection in China and Taiwan detracted from relative performance.

Market review and outlook

Emerging-market stocks posted double-digit gains and outpaced the developed markets in the quarter. After plunging in early April due to a shift in U.S. trade policy, the asset class quickly recovered as investors regained their appetite for risk. Expectations for falling interest rates provided additional fuel for the advance, as did pronounced weakness in the U.S. dollar. Together, these factors allowed the benchmark to finish June at its highest level in over three years. Latin American stocks performed particularly well amid the reduced uncertainty about trade policy, and the smaller European markets built on their impressive rally of the prior quarter. South Korea and Taiwan also advanced thanks to renewed optimism regarding the technology sector. Conversely, China lagged on concerns about the nation's economic outlook and the effects of tariffs.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection was the primary detractor from results, with the largest impact occurring in China. Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) and Trip.com, Ltd., both of which faced rising competition from JD.com (JD) (OTCPK:JDCMF), were the leading detractors. The fund was also hurt by a position in Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) (OTCPK:TCTZF) Ltd., which traded roughly flat after its strong performance in the first quarter, as well as Kanzhun Ltd (BZ). and Centre Testing International Group Co. Ltd., which were pressured by the worries surrounding U.S. trade policy. Stock selection in Taiwan detracted as well.

Selection in Brazil and India contributed, led by positions in MercadoLibre (MELI) Inc. and KEI Industries Ltd., respectively. The Korean semiconductor producer SK Hynix (OTCPK:HXSCF) Co. Ltd. was the largest individual contributor. The company has demonstrated clear leadership in high-bandwidth memory, which is in strong demand for use in Al.

Country allocation was a net positive contributor. An overweight in Greece helped due to strength in Piraeus Financial Holdings S.A. (OTCPK:BPIRF) (OTCPK:BPIRY) (OTCPK:BPIRF) and National Bank of Greece SA (OTCPK:NBGIF). An overweight in China and an underweight in Saudi Arabia further contributed, while underweights in Taiwan and South Korea detracted.

Portfolio changes

We actively managed the portfolio in response to the shifting economic environment and its potential long-term effect on companies' earnings prospects. In terms of positioning, we continued to favor Greece on the view that the country features undervalued growth with improving macro fundamentals, political stability, and strong reform momentum. We also maintained an overweight in India, where favorable demographics and structural reforms create the foundation for above-average economic growth. In terms of sectors, the fund was overweight in information technology, with an emphasis on semiconductor stocks and companies offering cloud-based software-as-a-service solutions, as well as in communication services and consumer staples. The fund was underweight in Saudi Arabia and other Persian Gulf nations based on slowing earnings momentum and limited transparency. It was also underweight in South Africa due to persistent structural challenges, policy uncertainty, and constrained growth prospects. At the sector level, materials, energy, and utilities were notable underweights.

Geopolitics and U.S. trade policy were key concerns in the first half of the year, but we believe the backdrop should remain constructive as long as tariffs aren't set at punitive levels. In addition, the weaker U.S. dollar provides an important tailwind for the asset class. We're therefore optimistic about the potential for emerging-market equities to outpace the developed markets over the remainder of 2025.

data.title: John Hancock Emerging Markets Debt Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Lower inflation, central bank interest rate cuts, and concerns about slowing global economic growth provided a favorable backdrop for EM debt in the second quarter, overcoming uncertainty about shifting U.S. trade policy.
  • Within EM, sovereign government bonds outperformed corporate securities, while high-yield bonds fared better than investment-grade issues.
  • The fund underperformed its benchmark, the J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index, due largely to country allocation and foreign currency management.

Market review and outlook

Emerging-markets bonds advanced in the second quarter against a backdrop of shifting U.S. trade policy. The enactment of new, wide-ranging tariffs by the U.S. administration early in the quarter led to lowered global growth expectations. Decelerating inflation and additional central bank interest rate cuts also contributed to the rally in EM bonds. Regionally, bond markets in Africa and Latin America posted the best returns, while Middle Eastern bond markets lagged. Within EM, bonds denominated in local currencies outperformed those denominated in U.S. dollars for the quarter. Meanwhile, sovereign government bonds outperformed corporate securities, and high-yield bonds outpaced investment-grade debt.

Looking forward, we remain cautious given heightened uncertainty around the economic backdrop, foreign trade policy, and ongoing geopolitical tensions. That said, yields on EM bonds are high by historical standards and should provide a buffer to help withstand short-term market volatility. In addition, lower interest rates and a weaker U.S. dollar should help ease inflation and give central banks more room to maneuver. In our view, EM debt should remain attractive to global investors searching for credit diversification. Given current valuations in EM bonds, we are taking a more tactical and selective approach to individual security selection.

Contributors and detractors

The fund posted a positive return for the quarter but underperformed its benchmark. Foreign currency management was one factor behind the underperformance, particularly short positions in the Mexican peso and Chinese renminbi. In addition, overweight positions in India and Brazil, along with a lack of exposure to Lebanon, weighed on relative performance, as did individual security selection in Brazil and Ghana.

On the positive side, overweight positions in Ecuador, Argentina, and Nigeria aided performance versus the benchmark, as did a lack of exposure to the United Arab Emirates. Security selection in India and Argentina also added value during the quarter.

Portfolio changes

We added local-currency bonds in Brazil, initiated new positions in Kazakhstan and Malaysia, and added modestly to the fund's corporate bond holdings in Mexico during the quarter. Meanwhile, we reduced the fund's exposure to localcurrency bonds in South Africa and India. From a currency perspective, we increased exposure to the Brazilian real and the Egyptian pound, added to our short position in the Chinese renminbi, and exited long positions in the Turkish lira, South African rand, and Indian rupiah.

data.title: 'May Cut' Isn't 'Will Cut', Expect More Volatility Ahead
data.description:
“May Cut” isn’t “Will Cut,” and other unknowns mean more volatility ahead.

I am saying there is a possibility, perhaps a strong possibility, that there is further volatility ahead and that there are a lot of ways the next few weeks could go wrong, even if we get as much as a ½ cut. I am going to try and cover the possibilities, but there are a lot of tangles in what could go wrong. And there are very few ways this could go right. We are going to have to thread a very tiny needle to get through the next few weeks without some selling. I expect that Q3 earnings will be a repeat of Q2, and the indexes should move higher off of those reports. So we have to get through September and the first weeks of October. Let me address the article’s title for a moment. In the next 3 weeks, the question of whether Powell cuts or doesn’t cut is really the catalyst for a bumpy market. As you will hopefully gather in the following paragraphs, there are a lot of decision points that create a lack of visibility. This is the crux of the issue: a quarter point in one direction is not nirvana, and it isn’t a cataclysm if there isn’t. The market badly wants one; at least that is what it seems, and that narrative could drive irrationality.

Powell could cut, and the market could still sell off

There are a number of macroeconomic data readouts going into mid-September. These milestones could provide cause for downward pressure. This week, we have the PCE (Personal Consumption Expenditures), the FED’s preferred inflation gauge. What happens if it shows more inflation than expected? We also have employment numbers for the month of August on September 5. Strong employment will be bad news. Strong employment means that the reasoning behind Powell’s possible decision to cut is unsound. As it stands right now, the cut is not an emergency; it is just an adjustment because inflation is less of a worry than employment. There could also be a lot of consternation from the media if we have a "one and done" hawkish cut, with Powell stating that there is no rush to add cuts, we have to balance inflation against job creation, and inflation is still an issue.

Employment data provides a new unknown

I admit that it would have to be a big bump in employment for it to matter to the Fed cut, because we have discovered that the employment numbers have been very overstated. The BLS downward employment revisions are so massive that a weak employment number could be seen as a recession already forming. So bad news, might be very bad news. Will Powell give us a ½ point cut if the employment numbers are bad? That would signal that the cut was necessary because the economy might be in recession. We don't know, so a ½ point cut under such circumstances does not soothe the bears. If you recall recent news, the BLS subtracted a massive 258K jobs that were supposed to be created in May and June (cite), and July’s report just had 73K jobs added. If you were to subtract 100K jobs from the July report (for an additional adjustment), you would be losing jobs in July, and we would already be at the start of a recession. So for the bulls, do you want a high employment number like 200K, or even 150K? Or do you want another 73K for August? I think either way the bears win, that is because we have about 3 weeks before we know what Powell and the FOMC decide. It is this lack of knowledge that could cause the market to fall.

Remember “Long and Variable Lags,” it works in reverse, too.

The statement about “long and variable lags” was coined by Milton Friedman (cite) to explain why inflation can move higher in the face of rising interest rates. Jay Powell used this concept to prepare economists and the business community that it will take time for inflation to be tamed. Now, the notion that cutting rates will take time to have some effect on a slowing economy will also take time. This is the time we may not have if we face the possibility of recession. If we do see signs of slowing, a ¼ cut is not going to hold back an economic slowdown. Dare I say it will be “Too Late.” The narrative that Powell has waited too long to cut sits in the back of the mind of every market participant. I am a Powell supporter, and as I look at the facts, I am beginning to wonder whether it is “too late.” Long-time loyal readers might remember that I was bullish even as Powell was raising rates, saying that the economy would prove resilient. I still don’t come out on the side of the bears here that we are going into a recession. I just think that the confusion caused by the tariff chaos, combined with the higher productivity, is slowing job creation. However, that is all the bears need to press the “stagflation” and even recession buttons.

There are just too many permutations on how the market could take the data and conjure negative scenarios. I am saying negative because the near future is unclear. Market participants hate a lack of clarity. When confronted with an unknowable possibility, it discounts the worst option. In short, markets hate opacity and will show that displeasure by selling off stocks.

The market loves the idea of rate cuts as long as they aren't needed.

We saw a very ebullient market reaction to Powell's Jackson Hole address because he appeared to be dovish. I heard “May” cut rates, which means he might not; if inflation is strong and employment for August rises strongly, then no cuts. Even if employment is soft, a cut is still an open question if inflation is higher. We won't really know until September 17 for the FOMC statement. This week will bring Fedspeak; the Fed Presidents are no longer speaking with one voice. Different Fed Presidents' opinions of whether to cut or not could very well roil the markets. What if, as I said earlier, we see weak employment and stubborn inflation? There are just too many questions to really project any kind of positive picture. The lack of opacity is acute, especially when we have to wait for 3 weeks in order to receive it. I can’t stress this enough; it isn’t whether we are in stagflation, recession, or everything is hunkydory. The fact is, we are waiting for a clearer picture, and we are counting on the next FOMC meeting to give us that. So in all the scenarios presented, I am merely trying to convey that there are too many unknowns and the market won’t like it one bit. That is the crux of the matter.

I believe that the best AI stocks are already telling us to “batten down the hatches.”

On August 10, I showed a chart of $PLTR, predicting that the stock would roll over because patterns often repeat (link to said article). Here is that chart

Here is the most recent 1-year chart of PLTR, with some new lines added

We now see that patterns do repeat, and I wasn’t making stuff up. PLTR has clearly rolled over and is now hopefully falling into support. The 2 parallel lines mark out what we call “congestion”; basically, this shows a wide band of support. PLTR could find support at that initial 145-ish level, or it could settle at the lower line, at 125. I think it falls to the 125, or maybe the 118 level. Why? You see the prior rollover at the lower left? Underneath it, I drew a "U." The low that day was 66. If you count the points, it dropped about 59 points from that prior high. If you subtract 59 from PLTR’s 52-week high of 190, you get to 130-ish. That is close enough for me. The reason I take it a bit lower than 125 is that there is firmer support under that 125 level. I will likely start accumulating at 130.

Let’s look at the biggest fish, which happens to report next week. Market participants have been nitpicky about the best-performing names, and I believe that NVIDIA (NVDA) will be no different.

Don’t get me wrong, I have all the confidence in NVDA. If NVDA does drop significantly, I would jump right in. All I am saying is that the lack of visibility into what happens next, I believe, will invite profit-taking, and NVDA could be prime for some selling. That could also be a catalyst for the other AI names if this little thing, of whatever the excuse is to sell, has a reflection on AI demand overall. Here is the 3-month chart of NVDA. I see an emerging roll-over formation already starting.

Here is a year-to-date chart of Nvidia showing that this stock can fall, and fall substantially, any time the AI juggernaut is in question.

I extended the horizontal line from the above chart to show this longstanding level. I believe that NVDA is vulnerable to 140; if this level doesn’t hold, 125 is the next test. If you think I am cherry picking, here is another AI titan: Meta Platforms (META). The stock has already seriously rolled over. I drew in an oval to illustrate what is called a “gap up.” Gap formations tend to get filled, and I believe META’s drawdown will fill it. The horizontal line is also where I see support so the META chart is telling us that we could see the stock at or close to 700.

What is the purpose of my showing you all these AI stock titans?

What I am trying to show is that all these very strong stocks are telling us that they are vulnerable to further price depreciation. This would line up with the lack of policy clarity when it comes to interest rates. Is the economy too hot? Or is it too cold? Is it already in recession, and a ¼ or even a ½ point cut too late? If PLTR, NVDA, and META are all showing weakness and a nascent rollover, won’t the rest of the market roll over?

The irony is that if we do have a slowing economy, the best stocks to be in are the secular growth stocks like the big-cap techs that are building out AI right now.

It is still possible that the market keeps chugging along

Look, it is very easy to be a bear; being negative makes one appear so wise when everyone is celebrating this cut that we may not need. So maybe the PCE comes in light, and employment for August goes to 150K, double July’s result. Also, everyone puts aside the BLS fiasco, and NVDA beats the pants off of expectations. Also, all the Fed Presidents agree that inflation is not an issue, so it is okay to start cutting. It all could happen. If that does come to pass, I will celebrate, because I am about 95% long. Still, I have come to respect the charts, and the picture they draw is not pretty at all. I want to stress that I don’t see a prolonged bear market; I just see a healthy retrace to get some froth out of the market. As I said earlier, I expect Q3 earnings to be quite strong, and that should get the stock market humming along once again.

What to do

Take a good look at your portfolio. We are at the very top of the market right now. The market could go higher if earnings continue to grow. That said, at this very moment, stocks are at a peak. That means that unless you have a good reason for holding a stock that is not near its highs or has not gone anywhere in a year, perhaps now might be a good time to trim the position. If your reason for holding said stock is for the dividends, that should not be the only reason to hold a stock. There are plenty of growth stocks that offer a good dividend and also capital appreciation. I will be trimming stocks and setting aside the cash. I did some investing, and now I am at 5% cash in my investment account. I want to work it back to 7%, or maybe even 10%. I have hedges via options in my trading account. I have no trades set for the upside now. I also have inverse ETFs that I am using as hedges. As per my investment group, we believe that cash has a value all its own. There is no virtue in having every penny invested. Cash gives you flexibility in times like this to use market movement to your advantage. I have been saying this in different ways for the last few weeks. I am trying to replicate the success our investment group had in taking advantage of the early April sell-off. If this turns out to be the wrong call and we sail through the fall into December 31st, I am confident I will find new places to redeploy the cash at hand to take advantage of that should it happen..

My investments

Intuit (INTU) had an excellent earnings report, and they adjusted their forward guidance higher, but not high enough. The stock sold off more than 6%, and at that point it gave me the 20% discount I am looking for (well, 19.4%, which is good enough). I started a small position; if we do get a sell-off, I will add more at lower prices. CrowdStrike (CRWD) dropped to 415, which is about a 20% discount, so I started a position with the understanding that they report this week. I think they may have shown weakness because of Palo Alto Networks' (PANW) fine earnings report and their acquisition of CyberArk (CYBR). CRWD has a partnership with Zscaler (ZS) for a competitive technology. Perhaps there is concern that CRWD will make an offer on ZS to compete. Whatever the reason, CRWD has to provide a tip-top earnings report to defend its share price. I think it goes lower; I hope it goes lower. I would love to own a larger position of CRWD.

I don’t have trades, but I am hedging using options and also owning a pile of inverse 3X ETF of the NASDAQ-100 (SQQQ).

Ok, everyone, I hope the market opens up strong and stays strong. Nothing would make me happier than to be wrong on this one. That said, if we do open strong, I will start trimming right away.

data.title: John Hancock Alternative Asset Allocation Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Alternative assets, while experiencing elevated volatility, finished the second quarter in positive territory as a group.
  • The fund produced a modest gain and underperformed its benchmark, the HFRX Global Hedge Fund Index.
  • The majority of the fund's individual positions delivered positive total returns.

Market review and outlook

After an uneven start, most segments of the world financial markets rebounded to record positive total returns in the second quarter. Risk assets fell sharply in early April in response to Washington's tariff announcement, but investors soon gained confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. The markets were also helped by an improving global interest rate outlook, highlighted by anticipation that the U.S. Federal Reserve may resume monetary policy easing in the second half of the year. Risk assets recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major global indexes to all-time highs by the end of June.

Contributors and detractors

The fund uses three main strategies: absolute return, designed to have low sensitivity to fluctuations in the broader financial markets; alternative investment approaches, where the underlying managers use opportunistic techniques; and alternative markets, which include asset classes where the drivers of performance are often unrelated to those of traditional investments. The fund's weightings in the three categories were 61%, 30%, and 7%, respectively, at the end of June. The fund continued to hold a tactical 2% position in cash given the category's attractive risk/return profile.

Alternative investment approaches made the largest contribution to performance, led by Disciplined Value Long/Short Fund (Boston Partners) and Hedged Equity Fund (JP Morgan). Alternative markets also contributed, with allocations to infrastructure stocks and gold both delivering healthy returns. On the other hand, an allocation to commodities detracted modestly.

The absolute return portfolio posted a negative return, only the second time in the past 12 calendar quarters that this has been the case. A sizable loss for Virtus AlphaSimplex Managed Futures Strategy Fund, whose trend-focused strategy was hurt by the market volatility in early April, played a key role in the shortfall. Diversified Macro Fund (Graham) also lost ground, further weighing on results. The other five holdings in absolute return finished in positive territory, however, led by Tactical Opportunities Fund (BlackRock (BLK)).

Portfolio changes

We made two notable moves during the quarter. First, Seaport Long/Short Fund (Wellington) was replaced by Disciplined Value Long/Short Fund following the merger of the two funds. We also sought to provide a measure of downside protection by adding a total return swap (a form of derivative) linked to equities. We anticipate that this effort to mitigate risk should allow us to allocate the portfolio's risk budget to opportunities with greater potential upside, improving the fund's overall return profile.

We think the evolving market environment continues to support the case for alternative assets. Bonds appear less likely to provide the appropriate diversification versus equities given unsustainable U.S. government spending and the likelihood that inflation will stabilize at a higher level compared to the 2010s. We also believe equities, particularly the U.S. large caps that dominate the global indexes, have reached valuation levels that indicate lower total returns in the years ahead. Together, we think these factors underscore the continued importance of seeking return potential in areas outside of traditional asset categories.
